coredata.dc:title: Neutral red/carbon dots photodynamic disinfectant spray for sports mouthguards cleaning and perishable foods preservation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jiuna
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial contamination of medical devices and food poses critical threat to mankind's health. Although some antibiotics can kill bacteria, their mis/overuse in healthcare and agriculture has brought about serious bacterial antibiotic resistance. Thus, developing a convenient and rapid sterilization method becomes increasingly important. Herein, the nanocomposite NR@CDs was synthesized by the assembly of neutral red (NR) with carbon dots (CDs). Compared with NR, NR@CDs has stronger reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation ability. NR@CDs can photodynamically destroy both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria efficiently, and the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) is 45 μg mL−1. NR@CDs spray is an eco-friendly sterilizing agent, with favorable biocompatibility and water-washability properties. Just 1 min of irradiation, NR@CDs spray can quickly and accurately kill the bacteria on the surface of the mouthguards (MGs). As the NR@CDs aqueous solution sprays on cherries, it can effectively prolong the quality guarantee period of perishable foods. The present work provides an innovative strategy to solve the global bacterial contamination and antibiotic resistance problems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neutral red (NR)
Carbon dot (CD)
Photodynamic inactivation (PDI)
Disinfectant spray
Mouthguards cleaning
Food preservation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of NR@CDs spray for cherry preservation
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and NR@CDs
Application of NR@CDs spray for cherry preservation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs, inset: size distribution of CDs. (b) XRD spectrum of CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (d) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (e) Full-scan XPS spectrum of CDs. HR (f) C1s, (g) N1s and (h) O1s spectra of CDs. (i) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.
(a) TEM image of NR@CDs, inset: size distribution of NR@CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NR and NR@CDs. (c) Excitation and emission spectra of NR@CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of NR@CDs.

(a) The absorption spectra of DPBF with green light irradiation for different time. The absorption spectra of DPBF with NR (b) or NR@CDs (c) under green light irradiation for different time. (d) Time-dependent photobleaching of DPBF induced by 1O2 produced by NR or NR@CDs (200 μg mL−1) with green LED light. The generation of ROS in S. aureus (e) and E. coli (f) biofilms processed with NR@CDs (200 μg mL−1) without (-L)/with (+L) green light irradiation. The survival rate of S. aureus (g) and E. coli (h) under different treatments (n = 3, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001). Pictures of colonies of S. aureus (i) and E. coli (j).

Growth curves of S. aureus (a) and E. coli (b) under different treatments. CV staining assays of (c) S. aureus and (e) E. coli. The relevant biomass (d) of (c) and (f) of (e) (n = 3, ****p < 0.0001). Fluorescence images of the S. aureus (g) and E. coli (h) biofilms processed with PBS or NR@CDs (200 μg mL−1) + L/-L.
Long-term antimicrobial capacity of the NR@CDs spray toward S. aureus on different substrates: glass, chemical fiber fabric, cotton and paper.
Resazurin staining (a) and plate counting (b) results of the antibacterial test. (c) Quantitative analysis of plate colonies in (b), (inset: the shape of MG).

(a) The photographs of cherries under different processing. Pictures of the cross section (b) and bacterial colonies (c) of cherries on the 9th day. (d) Weight-loss rate of cherries during storage. (e) The pH of cherries under different processing on the 1st and 9th day. (f) The VC and (g) TPC of cherries under different processing on the 1st and 9th day (n = 4, ****p < 0.0001).
The preparation of NR@CDs antiseptic spray for MGs cleaning and food preservation.

head.ce:title: Neutral red/carbon dots photodynamic disinfectant spray for sports mouthguards cleaning and perishable foods preservation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial contamination of medical devices and food poses critical threat to mankind's health. Although some antibiotics can kill bacteria, their mis/overuse in healthcare and agriculture has brought about serious bacterial antibiotic resistance. Thus, developing a convenient and rapid sterilization method becomes increasingly important. Herein, the nanocomposite NR@CDs was synthesized by the assembly of neutral red (NR) with carbon dots (CDs). Compared with NR, NR@CDs has stronger reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation ability. NR@CDs can photodynamically destroy both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria efficiently, and the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) is 45 μg mL−1. NR@CDs spray is an eco-friendly sterilizing agent, with favorable biocompatibility and water-washability properties. Just 1 min of irradiation, NR@CDs spray can quickly and accurately kill the bacteria on the surface of the mouthguards (MGs). As the NR@CDs aqueous solution sprays on cherries, it can effectively prolong the quality guarantee period of perishable foods. The present work provides an innovative strategy to solve the global bacterial contamination and antibiotic resistance problems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Mouthguard (MG) is an intraoral device to prevent oral injuries in playing sports. Since MGs are often reused for a long time, their daily maintenance and cleaning are very important for the health of athletes [1,2]. If the players can't clean MGs appropriately, a lot of microbial populations invade MGs, which can bring about serious or even fatal infectious diseases [3–5]. To suppress the bacterial colonization on MGs, developing highly efficient disinfectant agents to destroy the attached microbes is extremely urgent.

On the other hand, tons of food especially perishable fruits and vegetables are wasted yearly [6]. Perishable foods are prone to harbor many kinds of microbes, for instance, Gram-negative (G (−)) and Gram-positive (G (+)) bacteria, when they are consumed raw, pathogen contamination will lead to disease outbreaks [7]. In addition, the dehydration, texture deterioration, respiration and senescence of such produces during the storage also affect their freshness and sensory quality [8–10]. Thus, exploration of a cost-effective approach to address these issues is crucial to relieve food wastage.

In order to kill bacteria, multiple antibiotics come out successively [11–15]. However, their excessive consumption in healthcare and agriculture has resulted in the growing of antibiotic-resistant strains [16,17]. Antibiotic resistance and antibiotic pollution pose a great threat to mankind's health and environmental safety. Hence, it's of great significance to exploit efficient, eco-friendly and long-term bactericides to alleviate the hazards caused by bacterial contamination. Photodynamic inactivation (PDI) is a challenging technology that utilizing toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced by photosensitizers (PSs) with light irradiation to inactivate bacteria [18–20]. PDI exhibits a powerful antibacterial potency against a broad spectrum of pathogens, which can not only avoid bacterial resistance, but also facilitate to preserve the sensory and nutritional quality of food [21,22]. As the core component of PDI, PSs determines the safety and effectiveness of PDI. Therefore, various PSs have been investigated to achieve effective sterilization [23,24]. Unfortunately, the major disadvantages including poor water-solubility, weak antibacterial ability toward Gram-negative bacterial strains, tedious synthesis and high cost, inspire us to explore alternative PSs candidates.

Neutral Red (NR) usually acts as optical probes to achieve various applications in biology [25–28]. Nevertheless, little has been known about its photodynamic antibacterial efficacy so far. On another face, benefiting from the advantages of high hydrophilicity, good cell permeability and biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) [29–36] have been well recognized as ideal nanocarriers to deliver organic dyes [37], small-molecule anticancer drugs [38–43], and biomacromolecules [44–47]. In the present work, NR@CDs nanoantibiotic was fabricated (Scheme 1 ). In the green light exposure, NR@CDs possesses potent antimicrobial capacity against G (−) bacteria and G (+) bacteria, with minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 45 μg mL−1 and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of 60 μg mL−1. Moreover, a disinfectant spray was developed by dissolving NR@CDs in water, which facilitates large-area spraying onto different substrates and perishable foods. The antiseptic spray could inhibit biofilm formation and eliminate pathogenic microorganisms in the long run (at least 30 days). Furthermore, the NR@CDs spray could quickly kill the S. aureus adhering onto the surface of MGs, with a clearance rate of 100 %. In addition, the NR@CDs spray could also restrain the microbial growth, retard the respiration and dehydration of fruits, thereby prolong the quality guarantee period of fruits.

The aqueous solution of D-Glu (300 mg) was heated at 160 °C for 17 min. Then the solution (10 mL) containing 300 mg of ε-PL was added and carbonized for another 20 min. The obtained solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 3500 Da) for 6 h, filtered (0.45 μm) and lyophilized to obtain CDs.

CDs (10 mg) and NR (10 mg) were dissolved in 8 mL of methanol and stirred for 24 h. The resulting solution was dialyzed (MWCO: 3500 Da), filtered (0.45 μm) and lyophilized to obtain the NR@CDs powder.

NR@CDs were dissolved in water to obtain NR@CDs spray (80 μg mL−1). The corresponding NR concentration was 0.448 μg mL−1. The NR@CDs spray (2 mL) was sprayed on different substrates including glass, chemical fiber fabric, paper and cotton. The NR@CDs coated substrates were dried and stored away from light (room temperature). On the day 1, 4, 8, 12, and 30, two substrates of each type were taken of and incubated with S. aureus or E. coli suspension (1 × 105 CFU mL−1) for 20 min and irradiated for 10 min. One substrate was incubated with resazurin at 37 °C for overnight. The other was placed on agar plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The color of resazurin solution and the colony growth on agar plate were observed, the antimicrobial properties of different substrates sprayed NR@CDs were evaluated using resazurin method and agar plate method.

Transparent MG sheets were cut into 15 mm × 10 mm pieces with 3 mm thick. Prior to the experiment, the MG sheets were soaked in alcohol, cleaned with alcohol-tampon, dried and UV sterilized. Subsequently, the MG sheets were soaked in S. aureus suspension (10 mL, 1 × 105 CFU mL−1) and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The MG sheets were cleaned twice with PBS, then taken out and sprayed with NR@CDs spray (80 μg mL−1), then they were immediately illuminated by green LED light for different time (1, 3, 5, 7, and 10 min). By contrast, the MG sheets in the control group did not perform any processing. Finally, all of the MG sheets were placed into the centrifuge tube (50 mL) with 5 mL of PBS and incubated on a shaker for 5 min to collect the surviving bacteria on the surface of MG sheets, and the shaken suspension was applied to a plate for colony counting.

At the same time, the MG sheets treated in the same way were shifted to 6-well plates, and then LB medium (1 mL) and resazurin staining solution (0.5 mL, 0.2 mg mL−1) were added, and incubated at 37 °C for overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NR@CDs spray for cherry preservation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the freshness preservation effect of NR@CDs spray (100 μg mL−1), cherries were selected as a fruit model. The corresponding NR concentration was 0.56 μg mL−1. The fresh cherries were grouped into four groups at random (5 cherries per group), including NR@CDs + L, NR@CDs-L, PBS + L and PBS-L. The aqueous solution of NR@CDs was splashed onto the surface of the cherries with a hand sprayer, and the +L groups were illuminated with green LED light (18 mW cm−3) for 10 min, and then dried naturally (room temperature) for 0.5 h. In order to guarantee the uniformity of spray, each cherry was sprayed with NR@CDs spray (1 mL) each time on 1st and 4th day, and the fruit was stored at room temperature for 9 days. The appearance changes and weight loss of the cherries were recorded every day. Vitamin C (VC) and total polyphenol content (TPC) in cherries were measured on the 1st and 9th day.

For the purpose of studying the weight-loss rate of cherries, cherry samples were obtained from each group and weighed daily. The weight-loss rate was calculated as follows:
W e i g h t
l o s s
r a t e =
m 0
‐
m
t
m 0
× 100 %
where m0 and mt represent the mass of the cherry on 1st day (g) and the mass of cherry after storage for t days (g), respectively.

Finally, the pulp change and pulp colony count of cherries in different groups were observed on the ninth day. The cross-section of cherries was photographed. In addition, 2 g of cherry pulp was taken from each group, added with 10 mL of PBS. The mixture was homogenized, shaken on the shaker for 30 min, sonicated for 5 min, vortexed for 1 min and centrifuged. Then, 100 μL of the supernatant was coated on a solid agar plate, incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Photographs were taken to record the growth of the colonies.

All the data were analyzed with GraphPad Prism 8 software and shown as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The unpaired t-test was applied to analyze the significant difference (p), and p < 0.05 indicated statistically significant (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and NR@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized via one-pot pyrolysis method, utilizing ε-polylysine (ε-PL) and D- (+) -glucose (D-Glu) as the precursors. The morphology of CDs was seen through transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Fig. 1 a), which was torispherical nanoparticles with the diameter of 9.40 ± 2.60 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1b) demonstrates two peaks at 23.2° and 34.7°, corresponding to the (200) and (004) crystal planes of graphite, respectively [48]. The zeta potential of CDs was +9.05 mV.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1c) reveals two absorption peaks at 200 nm and 306 nm. which belong to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively. The strongest emission appeared at 437 nm with 397 nm excitation (Fig. 1d). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum (Fig. 1e) reveals CDs were mainly composed of C, N and O elements. The high-resolution (HR) C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1f) depicts three characteristic peaks at 288.3, 285.8, and 284.8 eV, attributed to CO, C–O/C–N, and C–C/CC. In HR N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g), the peaks at 401.5 and 399.8 eV were attributed to N–H and N–C. The HR spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1h) was assigned to C–OH (533.1 eV) and CO (532.1 eV) [49,50]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1i) demonstrates that CDs had O–H (3441 cm−1), N–H (3127 cm−1), CO (1638 cm−1), C–N (1391 cm−1), and C–O (1086 cm−1) groups [51], which agreed with the XPS analysis.

NR@CDs were prepared by a simple assembly of NR with CDs. The NR loading rate of NR@CDs was 0.56 %, on the basis of the standardized curve of NR (Fig. S1). The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) indicates that NR@CDs were spheroidal and well dispersed, and the average diameter was 32.20 ± 9.26 nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, NR has two absorption peaks at 274 and 526 nm, and NR@CDs has three absorption peaks at 200, 274 and 526 nm, indicating that the absorption peak of NR@CDs contains both the absorption peak of NR and the absorption peak of CDs, which proves the successful preparation of NR@CDs. The optimal excitation and emission of NR@CDs (Fig. 2c) was at 518 and 606 nm, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d) demonstrates that NR@CDs possessed O–H, N–H, CO, CN, C–N, and C–O bonds. The zeta potential of NR@CDs was +6.69 mV.

Subsequently, we investigated the 1O2 generation ability of NR or NR@CDs by utilizing 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) as a probe. Under light irradiation, the 1O2 generated by NR or NR@CDs reacts with DPBF to synthesize 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene, resulting in a significant decrease in absorbance. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the absorbance of DPBF without NR or NR@CDs did not reduce. By contrast, in the presence of NR or NR@CDs (Fig. 3b and c), as the illumination time increases, the absorbance of DPBF reduced gradually. Furthermore, the NR@CDs group showed the greatest reduction in absorbance (Fig. 3d), indicating that NR@CDs had the strongest photodynamic capability. ROS produced by NR@CDs in S. aureus and E. coli biofilms was evaluated by utilizing 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as an indicator. As displayed in Fig. 3e and f, there was no fluorescence in the PBS-L, PBS + L and NR@CDs-L groups, while strong green fluorescence could be seen in the NR@CDs + L groups, proving that NR@CDs could produce ROS in both G (+) and G (−) bacteria.

Inspired by the in vitro photodynamic performance of NR@CDs, we evaluated the antimicrobial capacity in vitro by determining the MIC and MBC. Antibacterial tests were completed utilizing G (+) S. aureus and G (−) E. coli as models. As depicted in Fig. 3g and h, the 96-well plate experiments showed that S. aureus and E. coli were obviously inhibited as the concentration of NR@CDs was 45 μg mL−1 with green LED light. The MIC of NR@CDs for S. aureus and E. coli was 45 μg mL−1. The plate counting experiments illustrated that colonies were completely killed when 60 μg mL−1 of NR@CDs was irradiated by green LED light for 10 min, proving that the MBC of NR@CDs was 60 μg mL−1(Fig. 3i and j). However, a great quantity of bacterial colonies was still present on the plates without light even though the concentration of NR@CDs reached 100 μg mL−1. In addition, the growth curve analysis showed that NR@CDs (100 μg mL−1) could entirely prevent the growth of S. aureus (Fig. 4 a) and E. coli (Fig. 4b) when the incubation time reached 6 h with light.

In addition, we also evaluated the antimicrobial capacity of CDs. As depicted in Figs. S2a and 2b, at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1, without/with light conditions, the bacterial viability of S. aureus dropped to 60 %, while that of E. coli remained above 80 %. The result absolutely approves that the germ-killing ability of NR@CDs primarily derives from the PDI of NR.

Once the biofilm forms, killing bacterial colonies will become much more difficult. Thus, preventing biofilm formation is extremely essential to removing bacteria. With this in mind, the capability of NR@CDs to prevent biofilm formation was assessed by crystal violet (CV) staining experiments. As depicted in Fig. 4c–f, the biofilm formation was clearly observed in the PBS groups and NR@CDs-L group. In contrast, 94 % of S. aureus biomass and 91 % of E. coli biomass in the NR@CDs + L groups were inhibited, indicating that NR@CDs (45 μg mL−1) could inhibit biofilm formation in the aid of green LED light.

To further assess the antimicrobial capacity of NR@CDs, biofilm live/dead staining experiments were performed. NR@CDs-treated S. aureus and E. coli were dyed with SYTO 9 (green fluorescence, live bacteria) and propidium iodide (PI, red fluorescence, dead bacteria). The stronger red fluorescence represented a more severe harm to the bacterial cells. As displayed in Fig. 4g and h, the PBS-L, the PBS + L and NR@CDs-L groups showed strong green fluorescence and almost no red fluorescence could be seen after SYTO9/PI staining, which indicated that there were scarcely any dead bacteria in these groups. While the NR@CDs + L group showed strong red fluorescence, demonstrating a large number of bacterial died. The result further confirms that NR@CDs could photodynamically destroy both S. aureus and E. coli.

The morphology of S. aureus and E. coli after processing with NR@CDs + L was observed through scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. S3). The morphology of S. aureus and E. coli in the PBS groups presented an intact structure of cell membrane. Reversely, after processing with NR@CDs + L, severe cell membrane disruption and cellular crumpling were observed. Thus, the broad-spectrum antibacterial properties of NR@CDs were demonstrated by 1O2-mediated bacterial membrane rupture.

As displayed in Fig. 5 , after incubation with the bacterial solution overnight, the colour of the resazurin solution altered from blue to pink in the control groups, revealing that a great deal of bacteria multiplied. The presence of abundant bacteria could also be observed on the corresponding plates. As for the substrates treated with NR@CDs + L after storage for 30 days, no bacterial colonies grew, and the resazurin solution was still blue, indicating that all the four substrates coated with NR@CDs could inhibit the growth of S. aureus over the long term. NR@CDs sprayed glass, chemical fiber fabric and cotton showed excellent antibacterial ability toward E. coli (Fig. S4), even after 30 days of storage. The results mentioned above definitely demonstrate the NR@CDs antiseptic spray has chronic bactericidal capability toward S. aureus and E. coli.

The resazurin solution turns to pink after incubating with bacteria overnight. As depicted in Fig. S5, the color of resazurin solution is deeper than that of NR@CDs solution. Therefore, NR@CDs has almost no effect on the color change of resazurin solution.

In addition, we explored the biocompatibility of NR@CDs using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S6, NR@CDs showed outstanding biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity against L929 cell, suggesting that NR@CDs spray is a good candidate for antimicrobial application.

The MG sheets were incubated with bacterial suspensions, then sprayed with NR@CDs aqueous solution, and illuminated with green LED light for different time. At each time point, the obtained MG sheets were incubated with resazurin staining solution for overnight. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the control group's solution turned pink, elucidating the formation of numerous colonies. On the contrary, after sprayed with NR@CDs, just 1 min of irradiation, the bacteria were completely killed. The plate counting (Fig. 6b) assay also showed the similar result. A great deal of S. aureus colonies was found on the control group's plate. In contrast, almost no colonies were observed on the plates from NR@CDs spraying MGs, indicating that the NR@CDs bactericidal spray could exert its function instantly. Meanwhile, the total viable count (TVC) in Fig. 6b was quantified and shown in Fig. 6c, the result proves that there were no bacteria in the NR@CDs + L group. The findings proved that NR@CDs spray can quickly remove or kill the bacteria adhering to MGs, with a clearance rate of 100 %. The excellent antibacterial efficiency and water washability of NR@CDs spray renders the long-term maintenance of MGs more convenient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NR@CDs spray for cherry preservation
ce:section.ce:para: As a proof of concept, cherries were utilized as model to assess the effectiveness of NR@CDs spray in food preservation. As shown in Fig. 7 a, post storage for 3 days, the cherries in the PBS-L and PBS + L groups appeared decay and mold. On the 5th day, they were completely rotten. The cherries in the NR@CDs-L group, presented obvious decay and mildew on day 6. What is in sharp contrast is that the cherries in the NR@CDs + L group, showed the least ripening and the best appearance with no microbial damage, demonstrating the admirable antiseptic and preservation performance of NR@CDs spray.

Fig. 7b depicts the cross-sectional pictures of cherries under different processing on day 9. The results displayed that the cherries in the PBS-L, PBS + L and NR@CDs-L groups had different degrees of shrinkage and decay. On the contrary, the cherries in NR@CDs + L group possessed fresh flesh, plenty moisture and bright red color, revealing that NR@CDs was able to effectively delay the rotting and deterioration of cherries and extend their quality guarantee period.

The bacterial colonies on the cherries were collected and analyzed by plate count (Fig. 7c). On day 9, a large number of bacterial colonies proliferated in the PBS-L, PBS + L and NR@CDs-L groups. Otherwise, almost no colonies were found in the NR@CDs + L group, which could be attributed to outstanding photodynamic antimicrobial capability of NR@CDs. Meanwhile, the TVC in Fig. 7c was also quantified and shown in Fig. S7, the result indicates that there were no bacteria in the NR@CDs + L group.

When the storage period ended, the weight-loss ratio of the cherries was appraised (Fig. 7d). The weight-loss rate of cherries in the PBS-L, PBS + L, NR@CDs-L and NR@CDs + L groups was 52.7 %, 71.5 %, 36.7 % and 15.3 %, respectively. NR@CDs have highly effective photodynamic antibacterial activity and can prevent the rot, the nutrient and water loss of cherries caused by bacterial contamination. Thus, the cherries in the NR@CDs + L group had the minimum weight loss.

The pH of cherries in each group on day 1 and 9 was presented in Fig. 7e. On day 1, the pH of cherries was 4.13. On day 9, the pH in the PBS-L, PBS + L, NR@CDs-L and NR@CDs + L groups was 5.73, 5.85, 5.24 and 4.57, respectively. The potent antimicrobial activity of NR@CDs could prevent the bacterial growth and proliferation, thereby maintaining the acidity of cherries.

To evaluate the antioxidant capacity of cherries, the VC and TPC content of cherries on day 1 and 9 were examined according to the standardized curves of VC (Fig. S8a) and TPC (Fig. S8b). As shown in Fig. 7f, on day 9, the VC content in the PBS-L, PBS + L, NR@CDs-L and NR@CDs + L groups decreased from the initial 57 mg/100g to 28.5, 25.3, 27.3 and 45.2 mg/100g. Compared to the sharp drop in VC content of the former three groups, the VC content in the NR@CDs + L group was down slightly. On day 1, the cherries had a TPC content of 1.2 mg GAE/mL (Fig. 7g). After storage for 9 days at room temperature, the TPC content in the PBS-L, PBS + L, NR@CDs-L and NR@CDs + L groups reduced 0.56, 0.53, 0.59 and 0.87 mg GAE/mL. Nevertheless, the TPC content in the NR@CDs + L group was noticeably higher than that in the other three groups. These results reveal that NR@CDs spray could serve as an eco-friendly and economical antistaling agent to prolong the shelf life of fruits.

To sum up, we successfully fabricated NR@CDs nanocomposite with outstanding photodynamic antibiofilm and antibacterial capability toward S. aureus and E. coli and the MIC was 45 μg mL−1. A powerful bactericidal spray was easily prepared by dissolving NR@CDs in water, which is biocompatible and water washable. Moreover, the NR@CDs spray displayed long-term antimicrobial performance against both G (+) and G (−) bacteria on a variety of substrates. The NR@CDs spray can rapidly remove the bacterium adhering to the surface of MGs after light exposure for 1 min. On the other hand, as the NR@CDs aqueous solution sprays on cherries, it can effectively inhibit microbial growth and preserve the freshness and nutrient quality, thereby prolonging the shelf life of fruits. This robust NR@CDs disinfectant spray has a great potential in clinical devices cleaning and perishable foods preservation.
Jiuna Zhao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2024.102279.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Injuries in Dutch elite field hockey players: a prospective cohort study, Scand
